summary: Shapiro reveals a clever eBay shopping hack: searching for misspelled product names to find underpriced listings. He demonstrates that many eBay sellers can't spell their products correctly, resulting in fewer bidders and lower prices compared to correctly-listed items or Amazon. The post uses woodworking tools as examples, showing that a planer on eBay with correct spelling costs nearly as much as Amazon after shipping. The core economic insight is that efficient markets work against bargain hunters, so introducing inefficiency through misspelled searches creates opportunity.
key_points: eBay items with correct spelling get bid up to near-retail prices, negating the platform's bargain appeal
Misspelled listings attract fewer bidders, creating genuine deals through market inefficiency
Amazon's free shipping and reliability often make it competitive with or cheaper than eBay for correctly-listed items
Local classifieds can offer even better deals despite higher listing fees, due to lower competition
money_quotes: Don't search for planers. Search for planrs, plainers, playners… have a couple of beers and let your mind roam free.
It turns out that many ebay sellers are spelling-impaired, and their confusion is your gain.
The basic idea here is that efficient markets are the bargain hunter's enemy. The less bidders, the better.
Whether you need a sandr, joyner, resiprikating saw, or just a set of forester bits, don't forget to commune with your inner dyslexic.
Amazon is an actual company that's somewhat less likely to vanish in the middle of the night on a midnight trip to vegas with your benchtop planer.
key_insight: Market inefficiencies created by seller misspellings on eBay are exploitable arbitrage opportunities for savvy buyers.
